title: Understanding Transformers: A Business-Friendly Guide
author: AI Systems Engineering Team
excerpt: Transformers power everything from ChatGPT to translation apps. Here's how they work — explained without the math PhD.

content.text: Before transformers, computers read text word by word (like humans). This was slow because each word had to wait for the previous one to finish processing. Transformers changed everything by letting the computer read all words at once and figure out which ones matter for each other.

Imagine reading: 'The cat sat on the mat because it was tired.' When you see 'it,' you know it refers to 'cat.' Attention does exactly this — it lets each word look at all other words and decide which ones are relevant.

In business terms: attention is like a smart search engine inside the model. When processing 'customer churn,' the model can simultaneously pay attention to 'churn,' 'retention,' 'satisfaction,' and 'lifetime value' — all in one pass.

items: Parallel processing means faster training and inference — less compute cost
Long-range dependencies let models understand context across entire documents
Pre-training + fine-tuning means one model can serve multiple business use cases
Transfer learning reduces data requirements from millions to thousands of examples

content.text: A single transformer model trained once can power: customer support chatbots, document analysis, sentiment monitoring, translation services, and content generation. The upfront cost is high, but the marginal cost of adding new use cases is low.
This is why companies like Bloomberg (BloombergGPT), Salesforce (Einstein GPT), and Morgan Stanley built their own transformer models — the ROI compounds across multiple business functions.
